questions.stem: A topological space $X$ is compact when which condition holds?
options: Every open cover of $X$ has a finite subcover.
questions.explanation: The topological definition: $X$ is compact if every open cover admits a finite subcover. Closed-and-bounded is the Heine-Borel characterization specific to $\mathbb{R}^n$, not the general definition.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: An open cover of a space $X$ is a collection of open sets with which property?
questions.explanation: An open cover of $X$ is a family of open sets whose union is all of $X$. A finite subcover is a finite subfamily that still covers $X$; compactness demands one always exists.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The Heine-Borel theorem characterizes the compact subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ (standard topology) as exactly which sets?
questions.explanation: The Heine-Borel theorem states that a subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is compact if and only if it is both closed and bounded. Either condition alone is insufficient.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: If $f : X \to Y$ is continuous and $X$ is compact, what is true of the image $f(X)$?
questions.explanation: The continuous image of a compact space is compact: given an open cover of $f(X)$, pull it back through $f$ to an open cover of $X$, extract a finite subcover, and push forward. This generalizes the Extreme Value Theorem.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a Hausdorff space, every compact subset has which property?
questions.explanation: A compact subset of a Hausdorff space is closed. One separates a point outside the compact set from it using the Hausdorff property and compactness, producing an open neighborhood of the outside point that misses the set.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A closed subset $A$ of a compact space $X$ is which of the following?
questions.explanation: A closed subset of a compact space is compact. Given an open cover of $A$, adjoin the open set $X \setminus A$ to cover $X$, take a finite subcover, and discard $X \setminus A$ to get a finite subcover of $A$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is every finite topological space compact?
options: Yes, any finite space is compact.
No, finiteness is unrelated to compactness.
questions.explanation: Any finite space is compact: an open cover can have at most finitely many distinct sets used to cover the finitely many points, so a finite subcover always exists. This holds regardless of the particular topology.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the open interval $(0, 1)$ compact in the standard topology on $\mathbb{R}$?
options: No, the cover by intervals $(1/n, 1)$ for $n \ge 2$ has no finite subcover.
questions.explanation: The open cover $\{(1/n, 1) : n \ge 2\}$ covers $(0,1)$, but any finite subfamily only reaches down to some $1/N > 0$ and misses points near $0$. So no finite subcover exists, and $(0,1)$ is not compact (it is bounded but not closed).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the real line $\mathbb{R}$ (standard topology) compact?
options: Yes, every cover has a finite subcover.
No, the cover by intervals $(-n, n)$ for $n \in \mathbb{N}$ has no finite subcover.
questions.explanation: The cover $\{(-n, n) : n \in \mathbb{N}\}$ covers $\mathbb{R}$, but any finite subfamily is bounded and cannot cover the whole line. So $\mathbb{R}$ is not compact (it is unbounded).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The Extreme Value Theorem, in topological form, states that a continuous real-valued function on a compact space has which property?
options: It attains a maximum and a minimum value.
questions.explanation: If $X$ is compact and $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $f(X)$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}$, hence closed and bounded, so it contains its supremum and infimum. Thus $f$ attains a maximum and a minimum.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the closed interval $[0, 1]$ compact in the standard topology?
options: Yes, by the Heine-Borel theorem it is closed and bounded.
No, only finite sets are compact.
Only its rational points are compact.
questions.explanation: The interval $[0,1]$ is closed and bounded in $\mathbb{R}$, so by the Heine-Borel theorem it is compact. This is the prototypical compact set on the line.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A continuous bijection $f : X \to Y$ from a compact space $X$ to a Hausdorff space $Y$ is which of the following?
options: Only an open map, not a homeomorphism.
questions.explanation: A continuous bijection from a compact space to a Hausdorff space is automatically a homeomorphism. Closed subsets of $X$ are compact, their images are compact in $Y$ hence closed, so $f$ is a closed map, making $f^{-1}$ continuous.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is compactness preserved by homeomorphisms?
options: No, it depends on the metric.
Yes, compactness is a topological invariant.
questions.explanation: Compactness is defined entirely via open covers, so it is preserved by homeomorphisms: if $X$ is compact and $X \cong Y$, then $Y$ is compact. (It is preserved by any continuous surjection, in fact.)
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The product of two compact spaces, with the product topology, is which of the following?
options: Compact only if both are finite.
Compact only if one factor is a point.
questions.explanation: A finite product of compact spaces is compact. (This is the finite case of Tychonoff's theorem, which extends to arbitrary products.) For example $[0,1] \times [0,1]$ is compact.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Tychonoff's theorem states which of the following?
options: A countable union of compact sets is compact.
An arbitrary product of compact spaces is compact in the product topology.
A subspace of a compact space is compact.
questions.explanation: Tychonoff's theorem asserts that any product (even an infinite product) of compact spaces, given the product topology, is compact. It is one of the deepest general results about compactness and is equivalent to the axiom of choice.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the set of integers $\mathbb{Z}$, as a subspace of $\mathbb{R}$, compact?
options: No, it is closed but unbounded, so by Heine-Borel it is not compact.
Yes, since each point is isolated.
Only the nonnegative integers are compact.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Z}$ is closed in $\mathbb{R}$ but unbounded, so by Heine-Borel it is not compact. Concretely, the cover by open intervals $(n - 1/2, n + 1/2)$ around each integer has no finite subcover.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A family of closed sets in a space $X$ has the finite intersection property (FIP) when which holds?
options: The whole family has empty intersection.
Every finite subfamily has nonempty intersection.
questions.explanation: A family has the finite intersection property if each of its finite subfamilies has nonempty intersection. Compactness is equivalent to: every family of closed sets with the FIP has nonempty total intersection.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Compactness can be characterized via closed sets as follows: $X$ is compact if and only if which statement holds?
options: Every collection of closed sets has empty intersection.
Every family of closed sets with the finite intersection property has nonempty intersection.
Every open set has compact closure.
questions.explanation: Dualizing the open-cover definition by taking complements gives: $X$ is compact iff every family of closed sets with the finite intersection property has nonempty total intersection. This is the FIP characterization of compactness.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Consider an infinite set $X$ with the cofinite topology. Is $X$ compact?
options: Yes; given any open cover, one set covers all but finitely many points, and finitely many more cover the rest.
No, the singletons form a cover with no finite subcover.
questions.explanation: Take any open cover and pick one nonempty member $U$; its complement $X \setminus U$ is finite. Each of those finitely many points lies in some cover element, so adding finitely many sets to $U$ covers $X$. Hence the cofinite topology is compact.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is a discrete space with infinitely many points compact?
options: No; the cover by singletons $\{x\}$ has no finite subcover.
Yes, every discrete space is compact.
Only if the space is countable.
questions.explanation: In an infinite discrete space, the cover consisting of all singletons $\{x\}$ covers the space, but no finite subfamily can cover infinitely many points. So an infinite discrete space is not compact. (A finite discrete space is compact.)
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which of the following subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ (standard topology) is compact?
questions.explanation: The set $\{0\} \cup \{1/n\}$ is closed (it contains its only limit point $0$) and bounded, hence compact by Heine-Borel. The half-open interval $(0,1]$ and $\mathbb{Q} \cap [0,1]$ are not closed in $\mathbb{R}$, and $[0,\infty)$ is unbounded.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The union of two compact subsets $A$ and $B$ of a space $X$ is which of the following?
options: Compact only if $A$ and $B$ are disjoint.
questions.explanation: A finite union of compact sets is compact: an open cover of $A \cup B$ covers each of $A$ and $B$, so it has a finite subcover for each; the union of these two finite subcovers is a finite subcover of $A \cup B$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a compact Hausdorff space, two disjoint compact subsets $A$ and $B$ can always be separated how?
options: By disjoint open sets $U \supseteq A$ and $V \supseteq B$.
Only if one of them is finite.
questions.explanation: In a Hausdorff space, disjoint compact sets can be enclosed in disjoint open sets. One first separates points, then uses compactness to pass from pointwise separation to a finite, and then global, separation. This underlies the normality of compact Hausdorff spaces.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X = \{a, b, c\}$ with any topology whatsoever. Is $X$ compact?
options: Yes, because $X$ is finite, so every open cover already has a finite subcover.
No, unless the topology is discrete.
It cannot be determined without the topology.
questions.explanation: Any space with finitely many points is compact: there are only finitely many points to cover, so any open cover trivially reduces to a finite subcover. The particular topology is irrelevant.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Classify several subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ (standard topology) for compactness using the Heine-Borel theorem. Consider $[0, 1]$, $(0, 1)$, $\mathbb{R}$, and $\{2, 4, 6\}$.
questions.solution: By Heine-Borel a subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is compact exactly when it is closed and bounded. Among the four sets $[0,1]$, $(0,1)$, $\mathbb{R}$, and $\{2,4,6\}$, the number that are compact is {{1}}. The finite set $\{2,4,6\}$ {{2}} compact, since finite sets are closed and bounded. The set $(0,1)$ fails because it is not {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $[0,1]$ and $\{2,4,6\}$ are closed and bounded, hence compact: that is $2$ of the four. $\{2,4,6\}$ is compact (finite). $(0,1)$ is bounded but not closed, so it fails compactness.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Apply the consequences of compactness. Let $X$ be a compact space and $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$ continuous. Reason about the image $f(X)$.
questions.solution: The continuous image $f(X)$ of the compact space $X$ is compact, so as a subset of $\mathbb{R}$ it is closed and bounded. Because it is bounded above and the supremum belongs to the set, $f$ {{1}} attain its maximum. The number of extreme values (a maximum and a minimum) that $f$ is guaranteed to attain on $X$ is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: $f(X)$ is compact, hence closed and bounded, so it contains its supremum and infimum. Thus $f$ attains both a maximum and a minimum, that is $2$ guaranteed extreme values (the Extreme Value Theorem).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Examine the open cover $\{(1/n, 1) : n \ge 2\}$ of the interval $(0, 1)$ in the standard topology. Decide whether a finite subcover exists.
questions.solution: Each set $(1/n, 1)$ is open, and as $n$ grows these intervals exhaust $(0, 1)$, so the family is a cover. Any finite subfamily uses a largest index $N$, and its union is the single interval $(1/N, 1)$, which omits all points in $(0, 1/N]$. Hence a finite subcover {{1}}. This shows $(0,1)$ is {{2}}. The number of points of $(0,1)$ left uncovered by the subfamily with largest index $N$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A finite subfamily reaches only down to $1/N > 0$, missing the infinitely many points of $(0, 1/N]$, so no finite subcover exists and $(0,1)$ is not compact.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Use the closed-subset and continuous-image principles. Let $K$ be a compact space, $C \subseteq K$ closed, and $g : K \to Y$ continuous.
questions.solution: A closed subset of a compact space is compact, so $C$ {{1}} compact. A continuous image of a compact space is compact, so $g(K)$ {{2}} compact. If moreover $Y$ is Hausdorff, then $g(K)$, being compact, is also {{3}} in $Y$.
questions.explanation: Closed subsets of compact spaces are compact, continuous images of compact spaces are compact, and compact subsets of Hausdorff spaces are closed. So $C$ is compact, $g(K)$ is compact, and $g(K)$ is closed in a Hausdorff $Y$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
